item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As others have said, the sudden extra members of the MTF were the wham factor for me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything was fine until

T5-7: Too long in the fire.

Jesus fuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, it's creepy as hell, and from an adhd mess like me my upvote is a huge compliment for a longer article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interface recording the audio feeds (which is likely in this scenario and with the Foundation as default) would be able to tell that voices were picked up that weren't mic'd.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I recommend linking the first mention of “Kervier” to SCP-2844, since that’s another article which heavily references the Kervier Mining Co.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so there are a few easy in-line links that can be made here. The first, is the conversion of the mention of Site-81 into a link to that site's dossier. Secondly, the mention of the Global Occult Coalition in the first addendum can be turned into a link to their hub. Finally, the mention of a Mr. Hedgewater in the fourth addendum is a direct refrence to a character at the center of SCP-2812, so his name can be turned to a link. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Note: The following information was gathered from the personal office of J. Howard Barnes, a Kervier administrator and Global Occult Coalition informant. After his death, the Foundation seized many of his assets, including information regarding the 1944 Lemon Quarry collapse which was thought to have been destroyed.

To this:

Note: The following information was gathered from the personal office of J. Howard Barnes, a Kervier administrator and Global Occult Coalition informant. After his death, the Foundation seized many of his assets, including information regarding the 1944 Lemon Quarry collapse which was thought to have been destroyed.

Changing this:

On June 4th, 1998, low level seismic activity was detected south of Site-81. A team of Foundation geologists were dispatched to gather more information relating to the event, but were unable to pinpoint the exact location of the disturbance. Anticipating aftershocks, several seismographs were installed in neighboring towns.

To this:

On June 4th, 1998, low level seismic activity was detected south of Site-81. A team of Foundation geologists were dispatched to gather more information relating to the event, but were unable to pinpoint the exact location of the disturbance. Anticipating aftershocks, several seismographs were installed in neighboring towns.

Changing this:

Ellis: Well, I hired in as a day laborer, just like my father had. He was a foreman with the company when I was a boy, so was my uncle, James Ellis. My uncle was an associate of Mr. Hoadley and Mr. Hedgewater, both big limestone men back in the 30s and 40s. I worked for Hedgewater for a while at United Limestone, and transferred to Hoadley just before my father retired, as a foreman myself.

To this:

Ellis: Well, I hired in as a day laborer, just like my father had. He was a foreman with the company when I was a boy, so was my uncle, James Ellis. My uncle was an associate of Mr. Hoadley and Mr. Hedgewater, both big limestone men back in the 30s and 40s. I worked for Hedgewater for a while at United Limestone, and transferred to Hoadley just before my father retired, as a foreman myself.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: One of my favorites..
item.content:encoded: While some people may not consider the logs or interviews really important, for me personally they definitely made the story. Part folklore, part history, all good stuff. Also the way you phrased everything, the way Mr. Ellis spoke, the words he chose helped to pinpoint what era he was from, I could clearly hear my elderly relatives from Missouri while I was reading it. This is one of my top 5. *sets up tent in the guides to learn to be so awesome* ;A;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of them came up the shaft. We tossed it back down. Wasn't right.

AUGH.

And when the additional "team members" started creeping in — good lord, I'm sure not sleeping tonight.

item.title: 10,000 days in the fire is long enough
item.content:encoded: The logs are really what make this article in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just cast my +1 into the fire pile.

For some reason I kinda held off on voting because I felt like there was some deeper meaning I "missed" the first time around (since i'm kinda used to kaktus skips having some sort of 'zinger' somewhere), but I've had the line "10,000 years in the fire" stuck in my head all day for whatever reason. So here it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My guess is that after the fact, the MTF members got interviewed and tried to recount what they said exactly, and then used trial and error to discern the differences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dig this, but question: if the "extra" members of the MTF are speaking with the same voices as the actual members, how does the person transcribing the audio know the difference? Like, if -7 is speaking with -3's voice, how would the transcriber know it's -7 and not -3 speaking?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Today I discovered that Bionicle is the flavor of the week for me_irl, and also I read this article. Both have enriched my life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddammit kaktus. You keep throwing amazing Kervballs at us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been reading SCPs for years now, but never made an account. I made an account immediately after reading this just so I could upvote it. This is the best thing I've read on this website, and I've read the entire Hall of Fame. I can't think of a single thing I didn't love. Brilliant writing, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Solid exploration, no surprise given the author. :) Have we seen Kervier before? I swear the name's familiar…

The best part is definitely when I noticed T5-7 was talking, went back up and discovered it wasn't a five-man team, but a four-man team. :D I like how subtle this is, too; it's never really stated what the anomaly is, though some obviously anomalous things go on in the addenda.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hit me up. I can see what I can do about getting some pics for you. The place is abandoned, you can just walk in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How long were we down there up here?

This is the exact point where I upvoted. It's not easy to express that concept in English.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anomalous hard rock mines - well, there's handful of buttons pushed for me. When I visited the Paris catacombs, being underground was more disturbing than the bones. This would be worse.
Now I'll be thinking about the local youth they never found.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same here. I saw the numbers six and seven and was like, "I thought that was a four man te-woooooaaaaah…."

Edit: I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotta say, you really know how to use the length of your articles to build suspense over an extended period of time. You'd think that with how many anomalies that have popped up in Indiana over the years the whole state was anomalous.

Either way, this was a solid article that creeped me out on more than a few occasions. I applaud the subtle introduction of T5-5, 6, and 7 in the exploration log. Very sneaky.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
abandoned mine near where I live

You lucky dog. I'd love to have some photo stock from that place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: T5-7 is when I subtly leaned away from my monitor as well :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A few of these I got off of Wikimedia commons, but the first two (the house and the cave in) I took myself at an abandoned mine near where I live.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good, I don't understand exactly what it is right now, but I'm not sure I'm supposed to. Just wondering, how did you get the pictures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminded me of 1408 in terms of atmosphere. That's a good thing, cause I absolutely adore that movie. I love how extra team members creep into the exploration log (I only noticed at -7, shame on me).

All in all a solid creepfest. +1

P.S. Hit me up about a collab, sailor. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a good, enjoyable read. You manage to make individual voices sing out in the dialogue, while also making it sound pretty realistic, which not many folks here can do well. The narrative is put together well, in that you've chosen a nice order to present the information in to keep us readers hooked from one section to the next. Oh, plus it's super creepy.

An easy +1, but with some little criticisms.

  • I'll agree that some of those addenda aren't doing much for the story. You could whittle away a few of those superfluous letters.
  • You maybe gave us too much mystery in the initial section of the description, especially with regards to what makes this thing anomalous. It's very vague. I totally get that you don't want to give everything away so we'll feel compelled to keep reading, but my response to that first bit was closer to confusion and slight frustration than being hooked in.
  • For a piece this long you have some of the inevitable grammary problemos, e.g. "fucks" when you should have had "fuck's [sake]". I also would quibble a few word choices, e.g. I'm not sure I would use "disparate" in that context, and I'm not sure I'd begin a footnote with "which". (But these are minor concerns, and the """tone""" overall is pretty bang-on.)
  • edit: containment procedures were quite bog-standard, but you get them out of the way quickly so they don't detract too much from the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like it, although the last addendum doesn't add anything. The "ten thousand years in the fire" definitely feels like the culmination to me. The description of the phenomenon in the exploration log and interview is what sells it, I think that's well written. The surrounding story with the mine owners etc also feels well though out - not too much, not too little.

So, there are immortal people down in the shaft, either the original lost miners or people who crawled out of the natural tunnel straight from hell. On at least one occasion something crawled up the emergency shaft. And one of the local villagers recently disappeared. You might think we need a perimeter to keep people out of the area. I say we need a perimeter to keep things in as well?

Local villager #4567: "Oh you guys really beefed up the security in the area, lots of dangers with a collapse eh? But who's that guy crawling down the hill, looks like he's on fire??"
Guard: "Don't worry about it, here have some candy."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't get me wrong, I like Kervier (2844 is proof of that) and would like to use them at some point in the future . It's just that the way the addenda are structured gives the impression that all the weird stuff like the thing crawling out of the shaft and the burning men only started happening once Kervier got involved.

Regardless, with the second exploration log removed I'll give this a tentative upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's definitely the mine's fault; the Kervier guys only become interested in it after the fact. They're mentioned a couple of other times in my articles, and it seemed like the right place for them to show up.

As for that second log, it's gone now. I already accomplished everything elsewhere anyway, it was just length at the end of the day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn dude, at this rate there won't be a mine in Indiana that isn't anomalous.

A couple things keep me at a neutral vote:

Addendum 4 (the interview with Gorman P. Ellis) isn't that great; it's basically pinning the blame for the haunted mine on the dudes from (and by extension) Kervier, when it's much creepier for the mine itself to be the root cause of the haunting.

Decibelles is spot on with her assessment of Addendum 6 - it doesn't differentiate itself from the initial exploration log already does, and the grunting and screaming from the Combustion Men could be appended into the initial log in 3 for a much sharper, pointed conclusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Look, Kak, as you know there are a lot of valid reasons I feel I might enjoy being a Foundation field agent moreso than being a boring retail worker with no life irl, but your articles always make me second-guess that wish. This is no exception. Yikes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not like I needed to sleep tonight or anything, kaktus. Eugh. Take your +1 and let me be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All fair points. I'll see what I can do about addressing those.

Edit: went ahead and ditched that second explo log. Better for it, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A really nice horrific article that feels like it's been dragged out by a couple of extra addena. The first addendum mentions collecting correspondence that J. Howard Barnes has. Both narratively and structurally (in-universe and out), it makes little sense that we'd get an addendum for that much later. The sixth exploration log was actually not as frightening or scary or even as creepy as the first one. It felt like it petered out somewhat when I think the most effective scary parts were all done in the first one (like the subtle use of introducing more MTF members).

item.title: 10,000 Years
item.content:encoded:
You'll notice a theme in a lot of things I write. Truth is, a lot of my family are from southern Indiana, and a lot of my family had business in and around mines. When you work in a business like that, you hear plenty of stories, and when you hear those stories as a kid, they stick with you. Become larger than life, even.

Anyway, this is something like that. All images are either from Wikimedia Commons, or my own. Many thanks to Jekeled for giving this a once over and helping me reach a more satisfying conclusion. Hope you all enjoy it.
